title: Organic Reactions Mastery
subtitle: Conditions, pathways, and Band 6 responses
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: write equations, state conditions and predict products to represent the reactions of hydrocarbons and alcohols
investigate the products of organic reactions including but not limited to: addition, substitution, oxidation, esterification and combustion reactions
inquiryQuestion: What are the products of reactions of hydrocarbons and how do they react?
lessonIntent: Students can recall every Module 7 reaction with exact conditions, avoid the most-penalised omissions, and structure a full-mark multi-step pathway response.
examSkill: Reproduce reagents and conditions from memory, choose the correct technique for each transformation, and write Band 6 pathway answers with equations, conditions and intermediates.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, organic reactions mastery, the conditions that win marks. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Organic Reactions Mastery (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 7, Lesson 20)

scenes.heading: Where pathway marks are really won
scenes.body: A band-six answer to a seven-mark pathway question carries seven equations, fourteen conditions, and seven named intermediates, all written cleanly under exam pressure.
scenes.callout: The chemistry is the easy part. The conditions are where marks live or die.
scenes.caption: Pathway marks come from conditions and intermediates, not just the reactions.

voiceover.text: OK, this is a consolidation lesson, and it's the most exam-focused one in the module. Picture a seven-mark pathway question. A band-six answer to it carries roughly seven balanced equations, fourteen sets of conditions, and seven named intermediates, all written cleanly under time pressure. Here's the thing students underestimate. Knowing which reactions to use is the easy part. The marks are actually won and lost on the conditions, the catalyst, the temperature, the pressure, distillation versus reflux. Today we'll pull every reaction in the module into one map, drill the conditions that get forgotten most often, and lay out how to structure a full-mark pathway response.

scenes.heading: Every reaction, one map
scenes.body: By now you have built every Module 7 reaction. Assembled into one map, the connections between functional groups become obvious.

bullets.text: Hydrocarbons feed in: alkenes add, alkanes substitute.
The alcohol is the hub connecting hydrocarbons to the oxygen families.
Oxidation climbs: alcohol to aldehyde to acid; then acid to ester.
Trace the shortest route, count the arrows, then add conditions.

scenes.callout: One map turns every pathway question into a navigation problem.
scenes.caption: The master map: hydrocarbons in, alcohol as hub, oxidation up to ester.

voiceover.text: Let's start by assembling everything into one map, because by lesson twenty you've built every reaction individually. Put the functional groups as points and the reactions as arrows, and the whole module fits on one page. The hydrocarbons feed in from the left. Alkenes react by addition, so they can become haloalkanes or alcohols. Alkanes react by substitution to give haloalkanes. The alcohol sits in the middle as the great hub, the bridge between the hydrocarbons and the oxygen-containing families. From the alcohol, oxidation climbs the ladder, alcohol to aldehyde to carboxylic acid, and then the acid plus an alcohol gives an ester. Once you can see the map, any pathway question becomes navigation. Find your start, find your target, trace the shortest route, count the arrows, and then the real work begins, adding the correct conditions to each step.

scenes.heading: The four most-penalised omissions

bullets.text: Alkene hydration: missing the high pressure. Catalyst and heat alone is not enough.
Alkane halogenation: UV is the energy source, not a catalyst.
Primary alcohol oxidation: distillation for the aldehyde, reflux for the acid.
Esterification: a reversible arrow and conc. sulfuric acid as catalyst.

scenes.callout: These four omissions cost marks in HSC almost every year.
scenes.caption: Most-penalised: hydration pressure, UV not a catalyst, distil vs reflux, reversible ester.

voiceover.text: Now the highest-value part of the lesson, the four omissions that cost marks year after year. First, alkene hydration. Students write the phosphoric acid catalyst and the temperature but forget the high pressure, and that drops a mark. Catalyst and heat alone is not enough. Second, alkane halogenation. The ultraviolet light is the energy source that starts the reaction, not a catalyst, so never label it as a catalyst. Third, and the big one, oxidising a primary alcohol. Distillation gives you the aldehyde, reflux gives you the carboxylic acid, and you must state which. Fourth, esterification. It needs a reversible arrow, not a single arrow, and concentrated sulfuric acid written as the catalyst above the arrow. Burn these four into memory. They're the difference between a complete answer and a frustrating string of dropped marks.

scenes.heading: When the technique is the answer
scenes.body: Several transformations share a reagent but differ only in technique. Naming the technique is what distinguishes the products.

bullets.text: Acidified dichromate plus distillation gives an aldehyde from a primary alcohol.
Acidified dichromate plus reflux gives the carboxylic acid instead.
Reflux means heating without losing volatile reactants, via a condenser.
Distillation removes a volatile product as it forms.

scenes.callout: Same reagent, different technique, different product.
scenes.caption: Distillation removes the product as it forms; reflux retains the volatiles.

voiceover.text: Let's go deeper on the single most tested distinction, where the technique itself is the answer. Acidified dichromate oxidises a primary alcohol, but what you collect depends entirely on the apparatus. Set up for distillation, and you collect the aldehyde, because the volatile aldehyde boils off and leaves the mixture before the oxidising agent can take it any further. Set up for reflux with excess oxidant, and you get the carboxylic acid, because the aldehyde stays in the flask and keeps reacting. So it's worth being crystal clear on what each technique does. Reflux means heating a mixture with a vertical condenser, so rising vapours cool and drip back, keeping volatile reactants in the flask. Distillation means deliberately boiling a volatile product out and collecting it elsewhere. Same dichromate, opposite outcomes, decided by the glassware. State the technique every time.

scenes.heading: Structuring a Band 6 answer
scenes.body: A full-mark pathway response has a fixed shape. Follow it and you never drop a structural mark.

bullets.text: Number each step clearly and write it in forward order.
Give a balanced equation, using structural formulae where asked.
State the full conditions: reagent, catalyst, temperature, technique.
Name the intermediate produced at each step, not just the final product.

scenes.callout: Number, equation, conditions, intermediate, for every step.
scenes.caption: Band 6 pathway shape: numbered steps, equations, full conditions, named intermediates.

voiceover.text: Now let's make your answers look like band-six answers, because structure earns marks on its own. A full-mark pathway response has a fixed shape, and if you follow it, you won't drop a structural mark. First, number each step and write the steps in forward order, start to target, even if you planned the route backwards in your head. Second, give a balanced equation for each step, and if the question asks for structures, draw full structural formulae showing every atom and bond. Third, state the full conditions, and that means all of them, the reagent, any catalyst, the temperature, and the technique like reflux or distillation. Fourth, name the intermediate you've produced at each step, not just the final product, because the marker wants to see you know what exists at every stage. Number, equation, conditions, intermediate. Repeat for every step.

scenes.heading: But-1-ene to butanone
scenes.question: Outline the synthesis of butanone from but-1-ene. Give equations, reagents, and conditions for each step.
scenes.coachNote: Butanone is a ketone, so you need a secondary alcohol. Hydration of but-1-ene gives butan-2-ol as the major product.
steps: Butanone is a ketone, so it must come from oxidising a secondary alcohol.
Step 1: hydrate but-1-ene. CH3CH2CH=CH2 + H2O, H3PO4 catalyst, ~300 degrees, high pressure.
The major product is butan-2-ol (the OH adds to the more substituted carbon).
Step 2: oxidise the secondary alcohol with acidified K2Cr2O7 under reflux.
CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3 oxidised gives CH3COCH2CH3, butanone (orange to green).
Answer: hydrate but-1-ene to butan-2-ol, then oxidise to butanone; the ketone is a dead end.
scenes.caption: But-1-ene hydrates to butan-2-ol, then oxidises to butanone (a ketone, dead end).

voiceover.text: Let's work a synthesis that tests several of these ideas at once, but-1-ene to butanone. Start by recognising the target. Butanone is a ketone, and a ketone comes from oxidising a secondary alcohol, so we need a secondary alcohol on the way. Step one, hydrate the but-1-ene. But-1-ene plus water, with a phosphoric acid catalyst at around three hundred degrees and high pressure, and note that high pressure, since it's the commonly forgotten condition. The major product is butan-2-ol, because the hydroxyl adds to the more substituted carbon. That's exactly what we want, a secondary alcohol. Step two, oxidise butan-2-ol with acidified dichromate under reflux, and it gives butanone, with the orange dichromate turning green. And remember, butanone is a ketone, so it's a dead end. There's no further oxidation. Two clean steps, full conditions, target reached.

scenes.heading: "UV light catalyses the chlorination"
scenes.body: Students label the UV light in alkane halogenation as a catalyst. It is not.
scenes.secondary: Ultraviolet light is the energy source that initiates the free-radical reaction by breaking the halogen bond. A catalyst lowers activation energy and is recovered; UV is energy that is absorbed, not a substance, so it can never be a catalyst.
scenes.callout: UV light is an energy source for the reaction, never a catalyst.
scenes.mistakeTag: UV initiates, it does not catalyse
scenes.caption: UV light initiates alkane halogenation; it is energy, not a catalyst.

voiceover.text: Here's a small wording trap that quietly costs marks. When students write the conditions for chlorinating an alkane, they often put ultraviolet light catalyst. That's wrong, and a careful marker will dock it. Ultraviolet light is the energy source that gets the reaction going, by breaking a chlorine molecule apart into reactive radicals to initiate the chain. Think about what a catalyst actually is. It's a substance that provides a lower-energy pathway and is recovered unchanged at the end. Ultraviolet light isn't a substance at all. It's energy that gets absorbed, so it can't be recovered and it can't be a catalyst. The correct phrasing is simply ultraviolet light, or UV light as the initiator, or as the energy source. Drop the word catalyst here, and you keep an easy mark that many students throw away.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: State the conditions to convert (a) propan-1-ol to propanal, (b) propan-1-ol to propanoic acid. Explain why they differ.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause and think about the technique for each before the answer plays.
answerSteps: Both use acidified potassium dichromate as the oxidising agent.
(a) For propanal, the aldehyde, use distillation to remove it as it forms.
(b) For propanoic acid, use excess dichromate and reflux.
They differ because distillation stops oxidation at the aldehyde stage.
Reflux keeps the aldehyde in the flask, so it is oxidised on to the acid.
scenes.caption: Both use acidified dichromate; distillation gives propanal, reflux gives the acid.

voiceover.text: Your turn, and this is the distinction that appears almost every year. State the conditions to convert propan-1-ol to propanal, and then to propanoic acid, and explain why they differ. Pause and think about the technique for each. Now let's check. Both use the same oxidising agent, acidified potassium dichromate, so the reagent alone doesn't separate them. The difference is the technique. For propanal, the aldehyde, you set up for distillation, which removes the volatile aldehyde from the flask as soon as it forms. For propanoic acid, you use excess dichromate and reflux. And the reason they differ is the whole point. Distillation pulls the aldehyde out before it can react further, stopping the oxidation at the aldehyde. Reflux keeps everything in the flask, so the aldehyde is oxidised on to the carboxylic acid. Same reagent, the technique decides the product.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Before you write a pathway, recall the four omissions and state the technique for every oxidation.
points: Assemble every reaction into one map, with the alcohol as the hub.
The four most-penalised omissions: hydration pressure, UV not a catalyst, distil vs reflux, reversible ester.
Where reagents match, the technique decides the product, so always state it.
Hydrate an alkene to a secondary alcohol, then oxidise to a ketone, a dead end.
Band 6 shape: numbered steps, balanced equations, full conditions, named intermediates.
scenes.caption: Master the map, the four omissions, and the Band 6 answer shape.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five takeaways. First, assemble every reaction into one map, with the alcohol as the hub linking hydrocarbons to the oxygen families. Second, memorise the four most-penalised omissions, the high pressure in alkene hydration, the fact that ultraviolet light is an energy source not a catalyst, distillation versus reflux in alcohol oxidation, and the reversible arrow with sulfuric acid for esterification. Third, where two reactions share a reagent, the technique decides the product, so always state it. Fourth, to make a ketone, hydrate an alkene to a secondary alcohol then oxidise, and stop, because the ketone is a dead end. And fifth, the band-six answer shape, numbered steps, balanced equations, full conditions, and named intermediates. Get the conditions right, and the marks follow. See you next lesson.
